primitives.name: Logistic Map
primitives.formalStatement: The logistic map is the discrete dynamical system x_{n+1} = r * x_n * (1 - x_n), where x_n in [0,1] and r in [0,4]. It exhibits period doubling, bifurcations, and chaos as r increases, serving as the canonical example of deterministic chaos.
primitives.computationalForm: Iterate x_{n+1} = r * x_n * (1 - x_n) from initial x_0. For r < 3: converges to fixed point. For 3 < r < 3.57: period doubling cascade. For r > 3.57: chaotic regime with periodic windows.

dependencies.description: Stability of fixed points determined by the derivative |f'(x*)| < 1
Discrete analog of continuous dynamical systems

compositionRules.yields: Classification of chaotic vs periodic regimes by sign of Lyapunov exponent
compositionRules.example: For r = 3.9: Lyapunov exponent ≈ 0.47 > 0, confirming chaos. For r = 3.2: exponent < 0, confirming stable period-2 cycle

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling population dynamics with carrying capacity
Demonstrating how simple rules produce complex behavior
Studying the onset of chaos through parameter variation

primitives.name: Lyapunov Exponent
primitives.formalStatement: The maximal Lyapunov exponent lambda measures the average exponential rate of divergence of nearby trajectories: lambda = lim_{n->inf} (1/n) sum_{i=0}^{n-1} ln|f'(x_i)|. For the logistic map, lambda > 0 indicates chaos; lambda < 0 indicates convergence to a periodic orbit.
primitives.computationalForm: For a 1D map f: iterate x_{n+1} = f(x_n) for N steps, compute lambda = (1/N) * sum_{i=0}^{N-1} ln|f'(x_i)|. Discard initial transient. For higher dimensions: compute from the Jacobian eigenvalues along the trajectory.

dependencies.description: Lyapunov exponent is computed from derivatives along trajectories
Continuous-time Lyapunov exponents involve integration

compositionRules.yields: Stability classification of continuous dynamical systems
compositionRules.example: Lorenz system at standard parameters has maximal Lyapunov exponent ≈ 0.91, confirming chaotic attractor

applicabilityPatterns: Quantifying the degree of chaos in a dynamical system
Predicting the time horizon for reliable forecasting
Distinguishing deterministic chaos from random noise

primitives.name: Feigenbaum Constants
primitives.formalStatement: The Feigenbaum constants delta ≈ 4.6692 and alpha ≈ 2.5029 are universal constants of period-doubling cascades. delta is the ratio of successive bifurcation intervals: delta = lim (r_n - r_{n-1})/(r_{n+1} - r_n). These constants are universal -- they appear in ANY unimodal map undergoing period doubling.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute successive bifurcation points r_1, r_2, ... for period-doubling cascade. Estimate delta_n = (r_n - r_{n-1})/(r_{n+1} - r_n). As n -> inf, delta_n -> 4.66920... This constant is the same for x^2+c, sin(rx), and any smooth unimodal map.

dependencies.description: Period doubling cascade is observed in the logistic map
Feigenbaum constants characterize bifurcation ratios

compositionRules.yields: Universal prediction of chaos onset from period-doubling parameters
compositionRules.example: For the logistic map, bifurcations at r = 3, 3.449, 3.544, 3.564... converge geometrically with ratio delta ≈ 4.669

applicabilityPatterns: Predicting the onset of chaos from observed period-doubling
Demonstrating universality in nonlinear dynamics
Establishing quantitative laws governing transitions to chaos

primitives.name: Strange Attractor
primitives.formalStatement: A strange attractor is an attracting set with fractal structure and sensitive dependence on initial conditions. The Lorenz attractor, generated by dx/dt = sigma(y-x), dy/dt = x(rho-z)-y, dz/dt = xy-beta*z, has a fractal dimension of approximately 2.06 and is the canonical example.
primitives.computationalForm: Integrate the Lorenz system: dx/dt = 10(y-x), dy/dt = x(28-z)-y, dz/dt = xy-(8/3)z using RK4 with dt = 0.01. Discard transient (first 1000 steps). Plot trajectory in (x,y,z) space. The butterfly-shaped attractor has positive maximal Lyapunov exponent.

dependencies.description: Strange attractors arise in continuous dynamical systems
Positive Lyapunov exponent characterizes strange attractors

compositionRules.yields: Quantitative characterization of attractor geometry
compositionRules.example: Lorenz attractor has correlation dimension ≈ 2.06, indicating a fractal object embedded in 3D space

applicabilityPatterns: Describing the long-term behavior of chaotic systems
Visualizing complex dynamics in phase space
Understanding weather patterns and turbulence

primitives.name: Bifurcation Theory
primitives.formalStatement: A bifurcation occurs when a small change in a parameter causes a qualitative change in the system's behavior. Types include: saddle-node (creation/annihilation of fixed points), pitchfork (symmetry breaking), Hopf (birth of limit cycle), and period-doubling (orbit stability change).
primitives.computationalForm: For dx/dt = f(x, r): find fixed points from f(x*, r) = 0. Check stability via eigenvalues of the Jacobian Df(x*, r). Bifurcation occurs where eigenvalues cross the imaginary axis. Track fixed points as r varies using numerical continuation.

dependencies.description: Stability analysis uses the Jacobian (matrix of partial derivatives)
Bifurcations occur when eigenvalues cross critical values
Bifurcation theory studies parameter-dependent differential equations

compositionRules.yields: Complete classification of local bifurcations via eigenvalue crossing patterns
compositionRules.example: Hopf bifurcation: complex conjugate eigenvalues cross the imaginary axis -> stable limit cycle born

applicabilityPatterns: Identifying critical parameter values where system behavior changes qualitatively
Classifying transitions between stable and unstable regimes
Designing systems that avoid or exploit bifurcation behavior

primitives.name: Sensitive Dependence on Initial Conditions
primitives.formalStatement: A dynamical system exhibits sensitive dependence on initial conditions if there exists delta > 0 such that for any x and any epsilon > 0, there exists y with |x-y| < epsilon and n > 0 such that |f^n(x) - f^n(y)| > delta. Informally: arbitrarily close initial states eventually diverge by a macroscopic amount.

dependencies.description: Positive Lyapunov exponent quantifies sensitive dependence
Local stretching rate determines sensitivity

compositionRules.yields: Prediction horizon: t_predict ~ (1/lambda) * ln(Delta/delta_0)
compositionRules.example: Weather prediction: with lambda ≈ 1/day and initial error 1mm, prediction breaks down after about 10-14 days

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding fundamental limits of predictability in chaotic systems
Quantifying the butterfly effect in weather and climate
Distinguishing deterministic chaos from stochastic randomness

primitives.name: Ergodic Theory
primitives.formalStatement: A dynamical system is ergodic if its time average equals its space average: lim_{T->inf} (1/T) integral_0^T f(phi_t(x)) dt = integral f d(mu) for almost all initial conditions x, where mu is the invariant measure. Ergodicity means a single long trajectory samples the entire attractor uniformly.
primitives.computationalForm: To test ergodicity: compute time average <f>_T = (1/N) sum f(x_n) along a trajectory. Compare with space average integral f d(mu). If they converge, the system is ergodic. Compute for multiple observables f.

dependencies.description: Ergodic theorem equates time integrals with space integrals
Invariant measure is a probability measure on phase space

compositionRules.yields: Statistical mechanics from dynamical systems: thermodynamic ensembles justified by ergodicity
compositionRules.example: Boltzmann's ergodic hypothesis: a gas molecule visits all accessible microstates, justifying the microcanonical ensemble

applicabilityPatterns: Justifying statistical averages in physical systems
Understanding long-term statistical properties of chaotic dynamics
Connecting trajectory behavior to probabilistic descriptions

primitives.name: Fractal Dimension
primitives.formalStatement: The Hausdorff dimension d_H of a set S is the critical value where the Hausdorff measure transitions from infinity to zero: d_H = inf{d : H^d(S) = 0}. For self-similar fractals with N copies scaled by factor r, d = log(N)/log(1/r). Fractals have non-integer dimension.
primitives.computationalForm: Box-counting method: cover the set with boxes of side length epsilon. Count N(epsilon) = number of boxes containing part of the set. The box-counting dimension is d = lim_{epsilon->0} log(N(epsilon)) / log(1/epsilon). Plot log(N) vs log(1/epsilon) and measure the slope.

dependencies.description: Fractal dimension extends the concept of dimension to non-integer values

compositionRules.yields: Quantitative characterization of chaotic attractor complexity
compositionRules.example: Koch snowflake: N=4 copies at scale r=1/3, so d = log(4)/log(3) ≈ 1.26

applicabilityPatterns: Measuring the complexity and space-filling properties of irregular shapes
Characterizing natural structures like coastlines, trees, and blood vessels
Quantifying the roughness or irregularity of a geometric object

primitives.name: Self-Similarity
primitives.formalStatement: A set S is self-similar if it can be decomposed into parts that are scaled copies of the whole: S = union_{i=1}^N f_i(S), where each f_i is a contraction mapping (similitude) with scaling ratio r_i < 1. Exact self-similarity holds for mathematical fractals; statistical self-similarity holds for natural fractals.

dependencies.description: Self-similar fractals have dimension determined by the similarity ratios

compositionRules.yields: Dimension calculation from similarity ratios: d = log(N)/log(1/r) for N copies at scale r
compositionRules.example: Sierpinski triangle: 3 copies at scale 1/2, so d = log(3)/log(2) ≈ 1.585

applicabilityPatterns: Identifying fractal structure in natural and artificial patterns
Generating complex geometry from simple recursive rules
Modeling phenomena that look similar at different scales

primitives.name: L-System
primitives.formalStatement: A Lindenmayer system (L-system) is a parallel string rewriting system: given alphabet V, axiom omega, and production rules P: V -> V*, apply all rules simultaneously at each step. Combined with a turtle graphics interpretation, L-systems generate plant-like fractal structures.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Start with axiom string omega. 2. Apply all production rules simultaneously to every symbol. 3. Repeat for n iterations. 4. Interpret result as turtle graphics: F = forward, + = turn left, - = turn right, [ = push state, ] = pop state.

dependencies.description: L-systems are formal grammars (sets of production rules)
L-systems generate self-similar structures

compositionRules.yields: Fractal plants with measurable dimension from L-system rules
compositionRules.example: Dragon curve L-system: axiom FX, rules X->X+YF+, Y->-FX-Y. After 10 iterations, dimension ≈ 2.0

applicabilityPatterns: Generating realistic plant and tree models procedurally
Modeling biological growth patterns
Creating fractal art and landscapes from simple rules

primitives.name: Cellular Automaton
primitives.formalStatement: A cellular automaton is a discrete dynamical system on a regular grid where each cell has a finite state updated simultaneously according to a local rule depending on neighboring cells. Wolfram's Rule 110 is proven Turing-complete. Conway's Game of Life uses a 2D grid with birth/survival rules B3/S23.
primitives.computationalForm: For 1D elementary CA: cell state s(i,t+1) = f(s(i-1,t), s(i,t), s(i+1,t)) where f encodes one of 256 rules. For Game of Life: cell is alive next step if it has exactly 3 live neighbors (birth) or 2-3 live neighbors and is currently alive (survival).

dependencies.description: State spaces and rule tables are finite sets
Some cellular automata produce self-similar patterns

compositionRules.yields: Computational universality from simple local rules
conditions: CA is Turing-complete (e.g., Rule 110, Game of Life)
compositionRules.example: Glider guns and logic gates in Conway's Game of Life can simulate any Turing machine computation

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling emergent behavior from simple local interaction rules
Studying computational universality in minimal systems
Simulating physical and biological systems on discrete lattices

primitives.name: Power Laws and Scale-Free Networks
primitives.formalStatement: A power law distribution has the form P(x) ~ x^{-alpha}, where alpha is the scaling exponent. Scale-free networks have degree distributions following power laws: P(k) ~ k^{-gamma} with gamma typically between 2 and 3. These networks arise from preferential attachment (Barabasi-Albert model).
primitives.computationalForm: To test for a power law: plot log(P(x)) vs log(x). A straight line with slope -alpha indicates a power law. Use maximum likelihood estimation: alpha_hat = 1 + n * (sum ln(x_i/x_min))^{-1}. For networks: generate via preferential attachment (new node connects to existing node with probability proportional to degree).

dependencies.description: Power laws are a class of probability distributions
Power law distributions satisfy probability axioms

compositionRules.yields: Scale-free fractal networks with measurable dimension
compositionRules.example: The World Wide Web has power law degree distribution with gamma ≈ 2.1, showing scale-free fractal structure

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling networks with hub-dominated connectivity patterns
Analyzing wealth distribution, city sizes, and earthquake magnitudes
Understanding why some nodes in networks become highly connected

primitives.name: Mandelbrot Set
primitives.formalStatement: The Mandelbrot set M is the set of complex numbers c for which the iteration z_{n+1} = z_n^2 + c (starting from z_0 = 0) remains bounded: M = {c in C : |z_n| <= 2 for all n}. Its boundary has Hausdorff dimension 2 and exhibits infinite self-similarity.
primitives.computationalForm: For each c in a grid of complex numbers: iterate z_{n+1} = z_n^2 + c from z_0 = 0. If |z_n| > 2 for some n <= max_iter, c is outside M (color by escape iteration count). If bounded after max_iter iterations, c is in M (color black).

dependencies.description: Mandelbrot set boundary is a fractal with dimension 2
Defined in the complex plane using complex arithmetic

compositionRules.yields: Infinite zoom revealing self-similar structure at all scales
compositionRules.example: Zooming into the boundary at any point reveals miniature copies of the full Mandelbrot set, demonstrating exact self-similarity

applicabilityPatterns: Visualizing the boundary between order and chaos in complex dynamics
Generating infinitely detailed fractal imagery
Studying the parameter space of polynomial dynamical systems

primitives.name: Small-World Network
primitives.formalStatement: A small-world network has high clustering coefficient (like a lattice) and short average path length (like a random graph). The Watts-Strogatz model interpolates between regular lattice (p=0) and random graph (p=1) by rewiring each edge with probability p. The six degrees of separation phenomenon is a small-world property.
primitives.computationalForm: Generate Watts-Strogatz: start with ring lattice of N nodes, k nearest neighbors. Rewire each edge with probability p. Compute clustering C = fraction of closed triangles among connected triples. Compute average path length L = mean shortest path over all pairs.

dependencies.description: Random rewiring uses probabilistic edge selection
Small-world and scale-free are complementary network properties

compositionRules.yields: Network models with both small-world and scale-free properties
compositionRules.example: Social networks are often both small-world (six degrees of separation) and scale-free (some people have vastly more connections)

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling social networks with high clustering and short paths
Understanding efficient information spread in networks
Designing resilient infrastructure networks

primitives.name: Turing Machine
primitives.formalStatement: A Turing machine is a mathematical model of computation consisting of: an infinite tape divided into cells, a head that reads/writes symbols and moves left/right, a finite set of states Q, and a transition function delta: Q x Gamma -> Q x Gamma x {L,R}. It defines the boundary of computability.
primitives.computationalForm: Simulate: read symbol under head, look up (state, symbol) in transition table, write new symbol, move head L/R, enter new state. Halt when entering an accept or reject state. A function is computable iff some Turing machine computes it.

dependencies.description: States, alphabet, and transition function are finite sets
Computability proofs use diagonalization and reduction

compositionRules.yields: Universal notion of computability: any effective procedure is Turing-computable
compositionRules.example: Lambda calculus, recursive functions, and Turing machines all define the same class of computable functions

applicabilityPatterns: Defining the fundamental limits of what can be computed
Providing a precise model for algorithm analysis
Establishing the theoretical foundation of computer science

primitives.name: Church-Turing Thesis
primitives.formalStatement: The Church-Turing thesis states that any function which is effectively computable (by an algorithm, in the intuitive sense) is computable by a Turing machine. This is a thesis, not a theorem -- it cannot be formally proved, but all known computational models have been shown equivalent to Turing machines.

compositionRules.yields: Equivalence of all reasonable models of computation
compositionRules.example: Lambda calculus (Church), mu-recursive functions (Kleene), Turing machines (Turing), and Post systems all compute exactly the same class of functions

applicabilityPatterns: Defining the boundary between computable and non-computable problems
Justifying the use of any convenient computational model for proving results
Understanding why programming languages are all fundamentally equivalent in power

primitives.name: Halting Problem
primitives.formalStatement: The halting problem is undecidable: there is no Turing machine H that, given any program P and input x, correctly determines whether P halts on x. Proof by contradiction using diagonalization: assume H exists, construct D that does the opposite of H's prediction about itself, leading to contradiction.
primitives.computationalForm: The diagonalization proof: Suppose H(P,x) = 'halt' if P halts on x, 'loop' otherwise. Define D(P) = loop if H(P,P) = 'halt', halt if H(P,P) = 'loop'. Then D(D) halts iff D(D) loops -- contradiction. Therefore H cannot exist.

dependencies.description: The halting problem is formulated in terms of Turing machines
Proof uses diagonalization argument

compositionRules.yields: Proof that many decision problems are undecidable via reduction to the halting problem
conditions: Problem can encode halting problem instances
compositionRules.example: Rice's theorem: every non-trivial semantic property of programs is undecidable, proved by reducing from the halting problem

applicabilityPatterns: Establishing fundamental limits of algorithmic problem-solving
Proving that certain programs cannot be automatically analyzed
Understanding why no general-purpose program verifier can exist

primitives.name: P vs NP Problem
primitives.formalStatement: P is the class of decision problems solvable in polynomial time O(n^k) by a deterministic Turing machine. NP is the class where a 'yes' answer has a polynomial-time verifiable certificate. P subset NP is obvious. Whether P = NP (can every efficiently verifiable problem be efficiently solved?) is the central open question of computational complexity.

dependencies.description: Complexity classes are defined via Turing machine time bounds
Polynomial time is characterized using Big-O notation

compositionRules.yields: Practical classification of problem difficulty: if any NP-complete problem is in P, then P = NP
compositionRules.example: SAT is NP-complete (Cook-Levin theorem). If SAT had a polynomial algorithm, P = NP

applicabilityPatterns: Classifying the inherent difficulty of computational problems
Determining whether efficient algorithms exist for verification problems
Guiding algorithm design: seek polynomial algorithms for P problems, approximate for NP-hard

primitives.name: NP-Completeness
primitives.formalStatement: A problem is NP-complete if: (1) it is in NP, and (2) every problem in NP can be polynomial-time reduced to it. Cook's theorem (1971) showed that Boolean satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete. NP-complete problems are the hardest problems in NP: solving any one in polynomial time would solve all of NP in polynomial time.
primitives.computationalForm: To prove problem X is NP-complete: 1. Show X is in NP (give a polynomial verifier). 2. Choose known NP-complete problem Y. 3. Give a polynomial-time reduction from Y to X: transform any Y-instance to an X-instance preserving yes/no answers.

dependencies.description: NP-completeness is defined relative to the class NP
Reductions are between Turing machine decision problems

compositionRules.yields: Practical algorithm strategy: use heuristics and approximation for NP-complete problems
conditions: Exact solution not feasible for large inputs
compositionRules.example: Traveling Salesman Problem (NP-complete): Christofides' algorithm gives 3/2-approximation in polynomial time

applicabilityPatterns: Proving that a problem is unlikely to have an efficient exact algorithm
Justifying the use of heuristics and approximation algorithms
Classifying problems into tractable and intractable categories

primitives.name: Big-O Notation
primitives.formalStatement: f(n) = O(g(n)) if there exist constants c > 0 and n_0 such that |f(n)| <= c * |g(n)| for all n >= n_0. Big-O describes an asymptotic upper bound on growth rate. Related: Omega (lower bound), Theta (tight bound). Used to classify algorithm efficiency.
primitives.computationalForm: To determine Big-O: find the dominant term as n -> infinity. Drop constants and lower-order terms. Examples: 3n^2 + 5n + 7 = O(n^2), 2^n + n^3 = O(2^n), n log n + n = O(n log n).

dependencies.description: L'Hopital's rule can determine asymptotic comparisons
Growth rates are measured as real-valued functions

compositionRules.yields: Complexity classification of algorithms by asymptotic resource usage
compositionRules.example: Merge sort: O(n log n) comparisons. Binary search: O(log n) comparisons. Matrix multiplication (naive): O(n^3)

applicabilityPatterns: Comparing the efficiency of different algorithms
Characterizing scalability of computational procedures
Abstracting away implementation details to focus on fundamental efficiency

primitives.name: Kolmogorov Complexity
primitives.formalStatement: The Kolmogorov complexity K(x) of a string x is the length of the shortest program (for a universal Turing machine) that outputs x. K(x) is uncomputable but provides a formal measure of information content. A string is random if K(x) >= |x| (incompressible).
primitives.computationalForm: K(x) cannot be computed algorithmically (proof by Berry's paradox / halting problem reduction). However, upper bounds are obtainable: K(x) <= |compressed(x)| + c for any compressor. A string x is considered random if no program shorter than |x| - O(1) produces it.

dependencies.description: Kolmogorov complexity is defined via Turing machines
Kolmogorov complexity is uncomputable (related to halting)
Algorithmic complexity provides an individual-sequence analog of Shannon entropy

compositionRules.yields: Connection between algorithmic and probabilistic information: E[K(X)] ≈ H(X) for random variable X
compositionRules.example: For a fair coin: each n-bit string has K ≈ n bits, and Shannon entropy H = 1 bit per symbol, so E[K] ≈ n = n*H

applicabilityPatterns: Defining randomness in terms of incompressibility
Measuring the inherent complexity of individual objects
Connecting information theory to computability theory

primitives.name: Neural Network
primitives.formalStatement: An artificial neural network is a computational graph: y = f_L(W_L * f_{L-1}(... f_1(W_1 * x + b_1) ...+ b_L)), where W_i are weight matrices, b_i are bias vectors, and f_i are nonlinear activation functions. A perceptron is the single-layer case: y = sigma(w^T x + b).
primitives.computationalForm: Forward pass: for each layer l = 1..L, compute z_l = W_l * a_{l-1} + b_l, then a_l = f_l(z_l), where a_0 = x (input). Common activations: ReLU f(z) = max(0,z), sigmoid f(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z}), tanh.

dependencies.description: Each layer applies a linear transformation (matrix multiply)
Inputs, activations, and weights live in vector spaces

compositionRules.yields: Trained neural network that approximates any target function
compositionRules.example: A 3-layer feedforward network with ReLU activations, trained by backpropagation on MNIST, achieves >98% digit recognition accuracy

applicabilityPatterns: Approximating complex input-output mappings from data
Pattern recognition in images, text, and audio
Building flexible function approximators for regression and classification

primitives.name: Backpropagation
primitives.formalStatement: Backpropagation computes the gradient of the loss function L with respect to all network weights via the chain rule: dL/dW_l = dL/da_L * da_L/dz_L * ... * da_l/dz_l * dz_l/dW_l. This enables gradient-based optimization of neural network parameters.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Forward pass: compute all activations a_l. 2. Compute loss L(a_L, y). 3. Backward pass: delta_L = dL/da_L * f'_L(z_L). For l = L-1..1: delta_l = (W_{l+1}^T * delta_{l+1}) . f'_l(z_l). 4. Gradients: dL/dW_l = delta_l * a_{l-1}^T, dL/db_l = delta_l.

dependencies.description: Backpropagation computes gradients for neural networks
Chain rule of calculus is the mathematical foundation
Matrix transpositions in the backward pass

compositionRules.yields: Complete neural network training pipeline
compositionRules.example: Backprop computes gradients, SGD updates weights: W <- W - lr * dL/dW, iterated over batches of training data

applicabilityPatterns: Training deep neural networks to minimize loss functions
Computing gradients efficiently through computational graphs
Enabling end-to-end learning from data

primitives.name: Gradient Descent
primitives.formalStatement: Gradient descent minimizes a function f(theta) by iterating theta_{t+1} = theta_t - eta * grad f(theta_t), where eta is the learning rate. Stochastic gradient descent (SGD) approximates the gradient using random mini-batches: theta_{t+1} = theta_t - eta * grad f_B(theta_t).
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Initialize parameters theta_0 randomly. 2. For each iteration: compute gradient g = grad L(theta; batch). 3. Update: theta <- theta - eta * g. Variants: momentum (v <- beta*v + g, theta <- theta - eta*v), Adam (adaptive learning rates per parameter).

dependencies.description: Gradient computation via backpropagation enables gradient descent
Gradient descent is the fundamental optimization algorithm
Gradient is the vector of partial derivatives

compositionRules.yields: Modern deep learning optimization with adaptive learning rates
compositionRules.example: Adam optimizer with lr=0.001 combines gradient descent with per-parameter adaptive step sizes and momentum

applicabilityPatterns: Training machine learning models by minimizing loss functions
Finding local minima of high-dimensional non-convex functions
Iteratively improving model parameters based on data

primitives.name: Loss Functions
primitives.formalStatement: A loss function L(y_hat, y) measures the discrepancy between model predictions y_hat and true targets y. Common losses: MSE L = (1/n) sum (y_i - y_hat_i)^2 for regression, cross-entropy L = -sum y_i log(y_hat_i) for classification, hinge loss max(0, 1 - y*f(x)) for SVMs.
primitives.computationalForm: MSE: compute (prediction - target)^2 for each sample, average. Cross-entropy: compute -target * log(prediction) for each class, sum over classes and average over samples. Gradient of cross-entropy with softmax output: dL/dz_i = p_i - y_i (elegant simplification).

dependencies.description: Loss functions evaluate neural network outputs
Cross-entropy loss is related to KL divergence between true and predicted distributions
Loss functions define the optimization objective

compositionRules.yields: Complete training objective: minimize average loss over training data
compositionRules.example: Training ResNet-50 on ImageNet: minimize cross-entropy loss between softmax predictions and one-hot labels using SGD with momentum

applicabilityPatterns: Defining what it means for a model to be good at its task
Connecting model outputs to optimization objectives
Choosing appropriate training signals for different problem types

primitives.name: Regularization and Overfitting
primitives.formalStatement: Regularization prevents overfitting by adding a penalty to the loss function: L_reg = L + lambda * R(theta). L1 regularization (R = ||theta||_1) promotes sparsity; L2 regularization (R = ||theta||_2^2, weight decay) prevents large weights. Dropout randomly zeroes activations during training.
primitives.computationalForm: L2: L_total = L_data + (lambda/2) * sum w_i^2. Gradient includes weight decay term: dL/dw = dL_data/dw + lambda * w. Dropout: during training, multiply each hidden activation by a Bernoulli mask with probability p. At test time, scale by p.

dependencies.description: Regularization modifies the loss function
Regularization is essential for training neural networks
Regularization constrains the optimization landscape

compositionRules.yields: Regularized training that generalizes to unseen data
compositionRules.example: Weight decay of 0.0001 in SGD prevents ResNet weights from growing unboundedly, improving test accuracy by 1-2%

applicabilityPatterns: Preventing models from memorizing training data
Improving generalization to unseen test data
Controlling model complexity to match available data

primitives.name: Universal Approximation Theorem
primitives.formalStatement: A feedforward neural network with a single hidden layer of sufficient width, using any non-constant bounded continuous activation function, can approximate any continuous function on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy: for any f and epsilon > 0, there exists a network g with ||f - g||_inf < epsilon.
primitives.computationalForm: The theorem is existential -- it guarantees existence but not efficient construction. In practice: increase hidden layer width until validation error is satisfactory. The required width may be exponential in dimension (curse of dimensionality), motivating deep (multi-layer) architectures.

dependencies.description: The theorem characterizes the expressive power of neural networks
Function spaces are vector spaces

compositionRules.yields: Practical universal function approximation via trained deep networks
compositionRules.example: A 2-hidden-layer ReLU network with 1000 neurons per layer can approximate most smooth functions encountered in practice

applicabilityPatterns: Justifying neural networks as flexible function approximators
Understanding why deep learning can solve diverse problems
Motivating the search for efficient network architectures

primitives.name: Convolutional Neural Network
primitives.formalStatement: A convolutional neural network (CNN) uses convolutional layers that apply learned filters across spatial dimensions: (f * x)(i,j) = sum_{m,n} f(m,n) * x(i+m, j+n). Weight sharing (same filter applied everywhere) reduces parameters and captures translation equivariance. Pooling layers reduce spatial resolution.
primitives.computationalForm: Convolutional layer: slide K filters of size (H,W) across input feature map, compute dot product at each position. Output channels = K. Add bias and apply activation. Max pooling: for each (p x p) region, output the maximum value. Typical architecture: [Conv -> ReLU -> Pool] x N -> Flatten -> FC -> Softmax.

dependencies.description: CNNs are a specialized neural network architecture
Convolution operation is the core operation
Convolution is a structured linear operation

compositionRules.yields: End-to-end trained image recognition systems
compositionRules.example: ResNet-50: 50-layer CNN with skip connections, trained on ImageNet (1.2M images), achieves 76% top-1 accuracy

applicabilityPatterns: Processing grid-structured data like images and audio spectrograms
Extracting hierarchical spatial features automatically
Achieving state-of-the-art image recognition and object detection

primitives.name: Attention Mechanism
primitives.formalStatement: Scaled dot-product attention computes a weighted sum of values V based on the similarity of queries Q and keys K: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) V. Multi-head attention runs h parallel attention heads and concatenates results. Self-attention allows each position to attend to all other positions.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Project input X into Q = XW_Q, K = XW_K, V = XW_V. 2. Compute attention scores: A = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)). 3. Weighted sum: output = A * V. Multi-head: repeat with h different projections, concatenate outputs, project with W_O.

dependencies.description: Attention is a neural network building block
Query, key, value projections are linear transformations
Softmax produces a probability distribution over positions

compositionRules.yields: Transformer architecture: the foundation of modern language models
compositionRules.example: GPT/BERT: stack of transformer layers (self-attention + feedforward), trained on massive text corpora, achieving state-of-the-art NLP

applicabilityPatterns: Modeling long-range dependencies in sequential data
Building transformer architectures for language and vision
Enabling dynamic weighting of input elements based on context

primitives.name: Reinforcement Learning
primitives.formalStatement: In reinforcement learning, an agent interacts with an environment in discrete steps: observes state s_t, takes action a_t, receives reward r_t, transitions to s_{t+1}. The goal is to find a policy pi(a|s) that maximizes expected cumulative reward: V^pi(s) = E[sum_{t=0}^inf gamma^t r_t | s_0 = s]. The Bellman equation: V(s) = max_a [R(s,a) + gamma * sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a) V(s')].
primitives.computationalForm: Value iteration: V_{k+1}(s) = max_a [R(s,a) + gamma * sum_{s'} P(s'|s,a) V_k(s')]. Q-learning: Q(s,a) <- Q(s,a) + alpha * [r + gamma * max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]. Policy gradient: theta <- theta + alpha * grad log pi_theta(a|s) * G_t.

dependencies.description: RL environments are Markov decision processes
Value functions are expected cumulative rewards
Policy optimization seeks reward-maximizing behavior

compositionRules.yields: Deep reinforcement learning (DQN, PPO, AlphaGo)
conditions: State/action spaces are large or continuous
compositionRules.example: AlphaGo: Monte Carlo tree search + deep neural networks for policy and value estimation, defeating world champion at Go

applicabilityPatterns: Training agents to make sequential decisions through trial and error
Solving game-playing and robotic control problems
Optimizing strategies in environments with delayed rewards

primitives.name: Bias-Variance Tradeoff
primitives.formalStatement: The expected prediction error of a model decomposes as: E[(y - f_hat(x))^2] = Bias(f_hat(x))^2 + Var(f_hat(x)) + sigma^2 (irreducible noise). Simple models have high bias (underfitting), complex models have high variance (overfitting). The optimal model minimizes the total error.
primitives.computationalForm: Bias = E[f_hat(x)] - f(x) (systematic error). Variance = E[(f_hat(x) - E[f_hat(x)])^2] (sensitivity to training set). Estimate by training on multiple bootstrap samples: compute average prediction (estimates bias) and prediction spread (estimates variance).

dependencies.description: Bias and variance are defined via expectations
Variance of the estimator drives overfitting

compositionRules.yields: Principled model complexity selection to minimize total error
conditions: Both training and validation error are monitored
compositionRules.example: Increasing L2 regularization strength increases bias but decreases variance; optimal lambda minimizes validation error

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding why model complexity must be matched to data quantity
Diagnosing whether a model is underfitting or overfitting
Guiding the choice of model architecture and regularization
